definition: A sub-class of Counterparty that applies to lending relations. The party that receives funds under a credit agreement and is obligated to repay.
An asset or property pledged by a borrower to secure a loan. If the borrower defaults, the lender may seize the collateral to recover losses.
An entity appointed to recover outstanding debts on behalf of the lender, typically engaged when internal collection efforts have been exhausted.
A sub-class of Borrower that applies to corporations. A legal entity that has entered into a credit agreement.
A loan extended to a corporate entity, typically with terms negotiated based on the corporation's creditworthiness and financial position.
A general party to a financial transaction or contract. In the NPL context, the entity that owes an obligation under a credit arrangement.
A set of counterparties that are connected through ownership, control, or economic interdependence, treated as a single risk unit for credit assessment purposes.
The legal process by which a lender exercises its rights to recover amounts owed, including seizure of collateral, court proceedings, or insolvency actions.
The outsourcing of debt recovery activities to a third-party collection agent or agency.
A concession granted to a counterparty experiencing financial difficulty that the lender would not otherwise consider, such as payment holidays, term extensions, or reduced interest rates.
A sub-class of Borrower that applies to natural persons. An individual who has entered into a credit agreement.
A licensed professional appointed to manage insolvency proceedings, including liquidation, administration, or voluntary arrangements.
An entity that provides insurance coverage related to credit risk, such as mortgage insurance or credit default insurance.
A financial instrument representing funds advanced by a lender to a borrower under agreed terms, including principal amount, interest rate, maturity, and repayment schedule.
A loan extended to an individual borrower, typically unsecured or secured by personal assets rather than real property.
Real estate or property pledged as security for a loan. The most common form of collateral for mortgage lending.
A credit rating agency that assesses the creditworthiness of counterparties, providing standardised risk ratings used in credit decisions.
A person or entity appointed by a court or creditor to take control of a debtor's assets during enforcement or insolvency proceedings.
